content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5602        322-5600           322-5901          322-4441            322-4444

March 15, 1982


Dennis Gahn
Kendel & Anderson
4000 MacArthur Blvd., Suite 100,
  East Tower
Newport Beach, CA  92660

                                      Re: Informational Request

Dear Mr. Gahn:

     Thank you for your recent telephone request for informa-
tion regarding Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18740. As we discussed on the telephone, if a filer wishes
to assert a privilege, he or she should make that assertion
with the filing of the Form 730. Simultaneously he or she
should seek approval for withholding otherwise disclosable
information, by making a request to the Executive Director,
Ted Prim. The matter will be handled pursuant to the provisions
of Section 18740.

     Per your request, I enclose copies of two advice letters
which we have issued relative to Section 18740; they should
provide some guidance to you. In addition, I enclose the
Feehan letter, page 4 of which may be of interest.

     If I can provide you with any further information,
please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                      Sincerely,

                                      Robert E. Ledigh
                                      Counsel
                                      Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosures:

January 25, 1982


H. Peter Klein
Deputy County Counsel
Courthouse
Ukiah, CA 95482

Dear Mr. Klein:

     I am writing in response to your request for information
from this office. You indicated in your letter that the
County of Mendocino's Conflict of Interest Code designates
the positions of County psychiatrist and psychologist.
Employees holding these positions are required to disclose,
in their Statements of Economic Interests, investments
and sources of income. These disclosure requirements are
intended to prevent the diversion of County mental health
patients to the psychiatrists' and psychologists' private
practices or to clinics in which they have a financial interest.

     Your concern is that these disclosure demands may im-
properly require the psychologists and psychiatrists to
communicate the names of patients and clients which are
ordinarily considered confidential and privileged. Similarly,
you are concerned that the County's disclosure provisions
for attorneys and social workers may improperly require them
to disclose their private clients' names.

     A private practice, such as one which may be engaged in
by a psychiatrist, psychologist, attorney or social worker,
constitutes a "business entity" for purposes of the Political
Reform Act. The Act defines the term "business entity" to
be "any organization or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership,
firm, ... corporation or association." (Government Code
Section 82005.) (See also Hays v. Wood, (1979) 25 Cal.3d
772,782-784.)

     Because a professional's practice is a "business entity"
under Mendocino County's Conflict of Interest Code and the
provisions of the Political Reform Act, a psychiatrist or
other professional with a private practice would only have
to report the names of clients from whom the professional
received a pro rata share of the fee (gross receipts) of

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Two


$10,000 or more during a calendar year. (See,Government
Code Sections 82030(a) and 87207(b)(3).) Since the pro-
fessionals designated in the County's Code probably have
very few private clients who provide them with $10,000 in
annual gross receipts, few, if any, names would be dis-
closeable under these provisions.

As to the names of those clients who do provide $10,000
or more, 2 California Administrative Code Section 18740
provides:

     An official need not disclose under Government
Code Section 87207(b)(3) the name of a person who paid
fees or made payments to a business entity if disclosure
of a person's name would violate a legally recognized
privilege under California law...."

Evidence Code Sections 1010-1026 recognizes a psycho-
therapist-patient privilege. The term "psychotherapist" is
defined in Section 1010 to mean:

     (a) A person authorized, or reasonably believed
by the patient to be authorized, to practice medicine
in any state or nation who devotes, or is reasonably
believed to devote, a substantial portion of his time
to the practice of psychiatry;

     (b) A person licensed as a psychologist...1/

     (c) A person licensed as a clinical social worker
... when he is engaged in applied psychotherapy of a
nonmedical nature;2/

     (d) A person who is serving as a school
psychologist...3/


_______________________

1/  The person must be licensed under Chapter 6.6 (commencing
with Section 2900) of Division 2 of the Business and Professions
Code.

2/  The person must be licensed under Article 4 (commencing
with Section 9040) of Chapter 17 of Division 3 of the Business
and Professions Code.

3/  The person must hold a credential authorizing such
service issued by the state.

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Three


(e) A person licensed as a marriage, family and
child counselor....

The patient-psychotherapist privilege is construed
liberally in favor of the patient and his right to privacy
(In re Lifschutz (1970) 2 Cal. 3d 415, 437) and it protects
the names of clients from disclosure (Smith v. Superior
Court (1981) 118 Cal. App. 3d 136, 141-142). The court in
Smith stated:

...When a patient seeks out the counsel of a psycho-
therapist, he wants privacy and sanctuary from the
world and its pressures. The patient desires in this
place of safety an opportunity to be as open and candid
as possible to enable the psychotherapist the maximum
opportunity to help him with his problems. The patient's
purpose would be inhibited and frustrated if his psycho-
therapist could be compelled to give up his identity
without his consent. Public knowledge of treatment by
a psychotherapist reveals the existence and, in a general
sense, the nature of the malady.

(At 142.)

Because of this patient-psychotherapist privilege,
disclosure of psychotherapists' clients' names is not required
on Statements of Economic Interest. As a result, the State-
ments cannot serve the County's stated purpose in designating
the positions of County psychiatrist and psychologist, which
is to prevent the diversion of county mental patients into
private practices and clinics. Unless there is another
purpose for these designations, the County should consider
removing or modifying them.

As to disclosure requirements for County-employed attorneys,
California law recognizes an attorney-client privilege (Evidence
Code Sections 950-962), although it is not as broad as the
patient-psychotherapist privilege and it does not ordinarily
protect the client's identity. (Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 Cal.
3d 772, 705.) Only limited protection of clients' names is

_____________________

4/ The person must be licensed under Chapter 4 (commencing
with Section 17800) of Part 3, Division 5 of the Business
and Professions Code.

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Four


provided by the privilege, such as in cases where disclosure
of the client's name might serve to make the client the
subject of an official investigation or expose him to criminal
or civil liability.  (Id.)

Under the County's Conflict of Interest Code, the names
of private clients providing a county-employed attorney with
$10,000 in annual gross revenues are required to be disclosed.
If an attorney feels that a particular client's identity is
privileged then he or she may apply to the Fair Political
Practices Commission for an exemption from disclosure under
the procedure outlined in 2 California Administrative Code
Section 18740.

In regard to the County's designation of social workers,
Evidence Code Section 1010(c) extends the psychotherapist-
patient privilege to include certain licensed clinical social
workers when they are engaged in applied psychotherapy of a
nonmedical nature. Other than this, there is no recognized
client-social worker privilege. The names of the clients of
these other types of social workers would ordinarily be
disclosable, subject, of course, to the $10,000 gross revenue
limit.

In conclusion, the $10,000 revenue limit and the applicable
privileges greatly minimize the disclosure of clients' names
under the Act. Therefore, Mendocino County may want to rely
on its local rules in order to protect against the diversion
of clients to private practices.

If I may be of any further help to you, please feel
free to contact me at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,


Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:km

sections.question_synthetic: Whether disclosure requirements in Mendocino County's Conflict of Interest Code for designated positions (psychiatrists, psychologists, attorneys, social workers) improperly require disclosure of confidential client and patient names, and how applicable legal privileges affect these disclosure obligations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The $10,000 gross revenue threshold and applicable legal privileges (psychotherapist-patient privilege, attorney-client privilege) significantly limit the disclosure of client names. Psychiatrists and psychologists need not disclose most client names due to the psychotherapist-patient privilege, but the County's disclosure designations may not achieve their intended purpose; attorneys and social workers have more limited privilege protections, though the $10,000 threshold applies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two letters dated March 15, 1982 (cover letter from Robert Leidigh) and January 25, 1982 (substantive response from Janis Shank McLean). The January letter constitutes the primary advice letter. No traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers are present; question and conclusion extracted synthetically from letter content. The March letter references two enclosed advice letters and the Feehan letter but these are not included in the provided text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether disclosure requirements in Mendocino County's Conflict of Interest Code for designated positions (psychiatrists, psychologists, attorneys, social workers) improperly require disclosure of confidential client and patient names, and how applicable legal privileges affect these disclosure obligations.

CONCLUSION: The $10,000 gross revenue threshold and applicable legal privileges (psychotherapist-patient privilege, attorney-client privilege) significantly limit the disclosure of client names. Psychiatrists and psychologists need not disclose most client names due to the psychotherapist-patient privilege, but the County's disclosure designations may not achieve their intended purpose; attorneys and social workers have more limited privilege protections, though the $10,000 threshold applies.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter responds to Mendocino County's concerns about whether conflict of interest disclosure requirements for designated positions improperly require disclosure of confidential client and patient names. The FPPC explains how applicable legal privileges and the $10,000 gross revenue threshold protect client privacy while addressing potential diversion of patients and clients to private practices.
